title: NATO's Ankara Summit Delivers $50B in Defense Deals, China Tests SLBM in the Pacific
subhead: NATO wraps its Ankara summit with $50 billion in procurement deals, a $40 billion drone initiative, and Patriot production licensing for Ukraine. Meanwhile, China fires a submarine-launched ballistic missile into the Pacific, and Russia's fuel crisis deepens as Ukrainian drone strikes force a diesel export ban.

sections.heading: NATO Ankara Summit: $50 Billion in Defense Deals and a Drone Edge Initiative
sections.body:
The NATO Defence Industrial Forum, held alongside the alliance's annual summit in Ankara on July 7-8, produced more than $50 billion in new procurements and investment packages, according to the Ankara Summit Declaration. The figure covers multinational procurement coalitions and industrial cooperation agreements under a new 'Made in NATO' framework.

The summit's flagship announcement was the NATO Drone Edge Initiative, a $40 billion commitment over five years to develop counter-drone capabilities and accelerate drone operator training. NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte said drones have 'fundamentally altered the character of modern warfare' and are becoming a 'decisive factor on the battlefield.' The initiative will create a dedicated counter-drone procurement market managed by NATO's Support and Procurement Agency (NSPA), with contracts worth hundreds of millions of euros. Allied nations also committed to increasing military drone operators fivefold by the end of 2027.

Rutte announced that member states had spent $37 billion on defense in one year and called for a 'revolution' in the defense industry. The alliance reaffirmed its commitment to the 5% of GDP defense spending target by 2035 — split between 3.5% for hard power and 1.5% for cyber and infrastructure. According to Reuters, only five of 32 members are projected to meet the 3.5% core defense spending target in 2026: Lithuania (5.33%), Estonia (5.1%), Latvia (4.92%), Poland (4.68%), and Greece (3.65%). Several members — including Albania, Slovenia, and Czechia — remain below even the old 2% threshold.
sections.whyItMatters: The $50 billion figure is a concrete signal that NATO is moving from spending pledges to procurement contracts. The Drone Edge Initiative is the alliance's largest single investment in a new warfare domain, directly shaped by Ukraine's combat experience. But the persistent spending gap between frontline states and southern/western European allies shows that the 5% target remains aspirational for most members.

sections.heading: ATACMS Production in Germany, Patriot Servicing in Europe, and Saab GlobalEye for NATO
sections.body:
Several specific defense industrial deals emerged from the Ankara forum:

Lockheed Martin and Rheinmetall signed a memorandum of understanding to establish ATACMS ballistic missile production in Germany — the first ATACMS production line outside the United States. Manufacturing will take place at Rheinmetall's Unterluss plant, with engine and component production potentially beginning as early as 2027. The move addresses chronic supply shortfalls for both Ukraine and NATO allies.

The United States, Germany, the Netherlands, Poland, and Sweden signed an intergovernmental agreement to explore establishing a dedicated PAC-3 missile service centre in Europe. Currently, Patriot interceptors must be shipped back to the US for maintenance, creating long downtimes. A European servicing facility would significantly reduce turnaround time.

NATO began formal negotiations to procure up to 10 Saab GlobalEye airborne early warning aircraft worth approximately $4.5 billion to replace the aging E-3 AWACS fleet. Denmark, Finland, Germany, and Norway signed a letter of intent for joint purchase of up to five MQ-4C Triton drones from Northrop Grumman for maritime surveillance.

Seven countries — Belgium, Croatia, France, Poland, Spain, Turkey, and the UK — launched a new High Visibility Project for shared Airbus A400M transport fleet operations. Finland also joined the multinational Airbus A330 MRTT tanker program.
sections.whyItMatters: The ATACMS production line in Germany is a landmark: it marks the first time the US has agreed to offshore production of one of its most sensitive missile systems, reflecting both the urgency of the Ukraine supply crisis and a broader shift toward distributing NATO's manufacturing base. The European PAC-3 servicing centre and the GlobalEye purchase signal that NATO is serious about replacing aging enablers with modern, distributed capabilities.

sections.heading: Trump Greenlights Patriot Production in Ukraine, Delays Turkey's F-35 Decision
sections.body:
President Trump told Ukrainian President Zelenskyy at the NATO summit that the United States would license Ukraine to produce its own Patriot missile interceptors. Zelenskyy confirmed the agreement, saying 'our teams must agree on all the remaining technical aspects.' However, Trump did not indicate whether any immediate Patriot supplies were on the table, and the manufacturers — Raytheon and Lockheed Martin — had not yet been informed of the decision.

Experts note that it takes approximately 24 months to produce a Patriot interceptor and about 30 months to manufacture its engine, according to the US Foreign Policy Research Institute. Ukraine's air defenses remain critically stretched: on the night of July 5-6, Kyiv's defenses failed to intercept a single ballistic missile — a worrying development for what had been the country's best-protected city.

Meanwhile, Trump indicated the administration would work toward lifting CAATSA sanctions on Turkey and reopening discussions about Ankara's return to the F-35 program, but no formal agreement was signed. The question of Turkey's F-35 access remains unresolved, with strong opposition in Washington citing Ankara's possession of the Russian S-400 system. NATO allies also committed $80 billion in defense aid to Ukraine for 2026.
sections.whyItMatters: The Patriot licensing is symbolically significant but operationally distant — Ukraine will not produce its own interceptors for at least two years. The immediate crisis of insufficient air defense persists. Turkey's F-35 question remains the alliance's most politically toxic procurement dispute, and Trump's vague signals do little to resolve it.

sections.heading: China Tests Submarine-Launched Ballistic Missile in the Pacific
sections.body:
On July 6, 2026, China's navy test-fired a long-range ballistic missile from a nuclear-powered submarine into the South Pacific, traveling approximately 7,300 kilometers from the South China Sea. The warhead landed in international waters outside Japan's exclusive economic zone but within the broader South Pacific Nuclear Free Zone established by the Treaty of Rarotonga.

Defense analysts identified the launch platform as a Type 094A SSBN and assessed the missile as likely a JL-2, though uncertainty persists because the JL-3's exact configuration remains unknown. The PLAN described the launch as routine annual training, but it drew protests from Japan, Australia, New Zealand, the United States, and Pacific Island nations. The US criticized China for providing only a few hours' warning before the launch.

According to analysts at the Fondation pour la Recherche Strategique and Naval News, the test signals China's transition toward a credible Continuous At-Sea Deterrence (CASD) posture. The launch demonstrated China's ability to conduct a second strike from protected 'bastions' in the South China Sea and Bohai Gulf — a fundamental shift from China's previous posture of primarily overland ICBM tests.

New Zealand Foreign Minister Winston Peters said the launch was 'inconsistent with the spirit and intent' of the Treaty of Rarotonga. Analysts anticipate more frequent SLBM launches and potentially coordinated triad exercises involving the PLA Rocket Force, PLAN, and PLA Air Force.
sections.whyItMatters: This is only China's second known full-range SLBM test in international waters, and it demonstrates a maturing sea-based nuclear deterrent that moves Beijing closer to sustained CASD alongside the US, UK, and France. The Pacific location — rather than the usual overland trajectory — is a deliberate signal to Washington and its regional allies at a time of heightened tensions over Taiwan and trade.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Drone Campaign Forces Russian Diesel Export Ban
sections.body:
Russia has banned all diesel fuel exports until at least the end of July, according to Russian state-controlled news agency RIA Novosti. The ban follows intensified Ukrainian drone strikes that have caused a domestic fuel supply crisis. Over the course of three nights, Ukrainian long-range drones struck 35 tankers, cargo ships, and special vessels in the Sea of Azov, according to Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces Commander Robert Brovdi. Separately, Reuters reported that the Saratov oil refinery halted processing after a drone attack on July 9.

Russian-occupied Crimea has been particularly hard hit. The proxy governor in Sevastopol, Mikhail Razvozhayev, said the city receives only a third of its daily fuel needs on average. Putin personally called on the Finance Ministry to provide fuel subsidies 'as quickly as possible' for Crimean residents.

The drone campaign has expanded beyond refineries to systematically target Russia's shadow fleet of fuel tankers used to supply Crimea and domestic logistics routes. Russia also announced it would allow refineries to produce lower-specification fuel for the domestic market to compensate for the shortages.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's systematic targeting of Russia's fuel logistics — from refineries to tankers to storage — has achieved what sanctions alone could not: an actual domestic fuel crisis forcing the world's largest energy exporter to ban its own diesel exports. The shift to targeting shadow fleet vessels shows how drone warfare is expanding from static infrastructure to mobile maritime targets.

sections.heading: Turkey Emerges as NATO's Defense Manufacturing Hub
sections.body:
Turkey's role as host of the NATO summit underscored its transformation from a problematic ally to a central pillar of European defense. Ankara announced $24 billion for its Steel Dome integrated air and missile defence system — a major indigenous investment. Turkey also allocated over $350 million for low-orbit satellites and military communications and $300 million for two high-resolution observation satellites developed by national institute TUBITAK.

Turkey and the UK signed a new security and defence partnership during the summit. Turkey, Romania, and Bulgaria expanded their Black Sea naval cooperation beyond mine-clearing to include protection of underwater cables, offshore energy, and pipelines. Turkish officials also pushed for access to EU defense financing mechanisms, arguing that NATO's security architecture should include all allies, not just EU members.

The Telegraph reported that Turkey has become 'the new pillar of European defence,' with its rapidly growing domestic defense industry — including Baykar drones and Togg armored vehicles — positioning it as a manufacturing hub the alliance increasingly depends on.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is leveraging its NATO host status and growing defense industry to rebrand itself from a difficult ally into an indispensable one. The Steel Dome investment and satellite programs signal that Ankara intends to build indigenous capabilities rivaling European systems — which could reshape alliance procurement patterns if Turkish firms gain broader NATO supply chain access.

sections.heading: Russia's Dronnitsa Forum Pivots to Preparing for War With NATO
sections.body:
Russia's annual Dronnitsa drone forum, scheduled for August, has set its keynote theme as 'preparing for a big war with NATO' — a shift from previous years focused on the Ukraine conflict. Samuel Bendett, a CNA/CNAS expert on Russian drones, noted the forum is moving from learning tactical lessons in Ukraine to practical preparation for a potential conflict with the alliance.

The forum has a track record of producing real capability: the KVN fiber-optic drone, immune to radio jamming, was a direct product of Dronnitsa brainstorming. Russia currently produces drones measured in the millions annually, while the US produces roughly 50,000 per year. Recent exercises suggest opponents fielding drones en masse could 'obliterate current Western forces,' according to one assessment cited by Forbes.

Russian military analysts have studied the US-Iran conflict closely, drawing lessons about how massed drone attacks can penetrate advanced air defenses and impose disproportionate costs. Russia has massive stockpiles of Iranian-designed Shahed drones and millions of tactical FPVs. Bendett also noted Russia has built a series of large new bases near the Baltics sufficient for an invasion force of over 100,000 troops.
sections.whyItMatters: Dronnitsa's pivot from Ukraine-focused tactics to NATO-war preparation is a concrete institutional signal. Combined with the Baltics base build-up and massive drone production advantage over the West, it underscores the urgency behind NATO's $40 billion Drone Edge counter-drone investment — and the real risk that Russia's current drone supremacy could be decisive in a rapid Baltics scenario.

bottomLine: The NATO Ankara summit delivered tangible defense industrial outcomes: $50 billion in procurement deals, a $40 billion drone initiative shaped by Ukraine's combat experience, ATACMS production shifting to Germany, and Patriot licensing for Ukraine. But the summit's achievements sit against a darker backdrop — China demonstrating a mature SLBM capability that advances its nuclear deterrence posture, Russia's drone industrial base pivoting toward NATO-war planning, and Ukraine's air defenses stretched to the breaking point with Patriot production at least two years away. The gap between NATO's spending commitments and actual delivery remains the alliance's persistent vulnerability: only five of 32 members meet the core defense spending target, and the alliance's counter-drone investment, while unprecedented, must catch up to a threat that is already being weaponized at scale.
sources: Reuters, Forbes, Defence24, TRT World, Naval News, AP News, Kyiv Independent, Strait Times, Shephard Media, Task & Purpose, DroneLife, Taipei Times, Bloomberg, NY Times, Al Jazeera, CBS News, The Moscow Times, Atlantic Council, Fondation pour la Recherche Strategique
